content.text: Every large language model—whether it is drafting prose, writing Python, or emitting a JSON tool call—is doing exactly one thing at its core: predicting the
next token

given all previous tokens. Understanding this autoregressive loop from first principles is the foundation for reasoning about agent latency, hallucination, structured output reliability, and every sampling parameter your orchestration code touches.
What it is
A decoder-only language model defines a probability distribution over sequences of tokens. The probability of a complete sequence
is factored using the chain rule:
At each step
the model runs a forward pass over all prior tokens and produces a raw score vector—
logits
where
is the vocabulary size (typically 32 000–200 000). The softmax converts logits to a proper probability distribution:
where
is the sampling temperature. At
the distribution collapses to argmax (greedy); at
logits are used unscaled; at

the distribution flattens. One token is drawn from this distribution, appended to the context, and the process repeats until an end-of-sequence token is produced or a maximum token budget is exhausted.
Why it matters

Every agentic capability—chain-of-thought reasoning, tool invocation, multi-step planning, structured JSON output—is implemented entirely through repeated next-token prediction. There is no separate "planner" or "tool-caller" module inside the model; what appears to be high-level reasoning is the emergent result of the model having learned, during pre-training, that certain token sequences follow others. This has three practical consequences for agent builders:
Every token in the model's reasoning trace consumes both compute and context budget, including "thinking" tokens that the user never reads.

Errors commit early: once a wrong token is sampled it becomes part of the next-step context, pushing subsequent predictions further astray—there is no backtracking unless the orchestrator explicitly retries.

Structured output (tool call JSON, YAML plans) works only if the model assigns high probability to valid continuation tokens; logit bias and grammar-constrained decoding are levers to enforce this at generation time.
Benefits
Unified pre-training objective: the same cross-entropy loss trains the model to produce prose, code, JSON, and tool calls from one identical objective.
Output format agnosticism: the LM head (a single linear projection) produces logits over the entire vocabulary, so the same model can emit any modality that can be tokenized.
Logprob access: generation APIs expose per-token log-probabilities, enabling confidence scoring, reranking of candidate outputs, and anomaly detection in agent pipelines.
Incremental streaming: because tokens are produced one at a time, the model can stream partial outputs to the user or orchestrator immediately, reducing time-to-first-byte.
Fine-tuning alignment: instruction following and RLHF-tuned alignment both operate on the same token-level objective; agents inherit these properties without architectural changes.
Trade-offs
No built-in backtracking: once a bad token is committed it is part of the prefix. Recovery requires external scaffolding (retry loops, tree search, or best-of-N sampling).
Greedy decoding can degenerate: without sampling noise, the model may repeat phrases or loop because the highest-probability continuation reinforces itself.
Each decode step requires a full (or cached) forward pass: parallelism within a single sequence is impossible during decode—new tokens depend on all prior tokens.
Hallucination as confident prediction: the model has no explicit world model; it assigns high probability to plausible-looking but factually incorrect continuations.

Context length pressure: every decode step must attend over the full prefix; long agent traces (tool call results, multi-turn history) increase both memory and latency quadratically without FlashAttention or sliding-window tricks.
How it works
Pre-training objective
During pre-training the model minimizes the average negative log-likelihood across every token position in the training corpus:

Gradient descent adjusts model weights so that, for every position, the distribution over next tokens concentrates on the actual next token in the training data. After training on trillions of tokens from diverse text, the model has implicitly encoded encyclopedic knowledge in its parameters—but it remains a next-token predictor at inference time.
Prefill phase
When a prompt (or prior conversation turns) is submitted, the model processes the entire sequence in one (or chunked) forward pass called
prefill

. During prefill all attention operations can run in parallel across token positions using matrix operations on the GPU. The key-value (KV) vectors for every prompt token are computed once and cached. Prefill is compute-bound and benefits from large batch sizes and fused attention kernels. For an agent with a large system prompt and accumulated tool results, prefill can dominate total latency.
Decode phase

After prefill the model enters the decode (generation) phase. In each step only one new token position is processed: the model attends to the full cached KV prefix plus the most recently generated token. The output logit at the last position is passed through softmax and a token is sampled. The new token is appended to the KV cache and the loop repeats. Decode is
memory-bandwidth-bound

—the bottleneck is reading large model weights and the KV cache from GPU HBM, not floating-point operations. This means decode throughput does not improve much with larger batch sizes beyond a certain point.
KV cache mechanics

Without the KV cache each decode step would re-compute attention keys and values for all prior tokens—O(n) work per step, O(n²) total. The KV cache stores computed key and value tensors per layer, per token, per head. Cache size for a modern 70B model is roughly
. A 128-layer, 128-head, 128-d-head model at 4 096 tokens uses around 4 GB of cache—relevant when batching many agents on shared GPU memory.
Sampling strategies
Four strategies are commonly used to convert logits to a sampled token:
Greedy (argmax,
): deterministic, fast, prone to repetition loops and local optima.
Temperature sampling: divide logits by
before softmax; controls the entropy of the output distribution.
Top-k: keep only the k tokens with highest logit, zero out the rest, then sample. Reduces the chance of selecting very low-probability tokens but k is a fixed hard cutoff.
Top-p (nucleus): keep the smallest set of tokens whose cumulative probability meets threshold p (e.g. 0.9); the nucleus adapts in width based on distribution shape, making it more robust than top-k.
Architecture
The diagram below shows the full autoregressive decode loop from prompt tokens to sampled next token, including the feedback path that appends each output token to the prefix:
The five stages—
Input Tokens → Embedding → Transformer → Logits → Softmax/Sample
—form one iteration of the decode loop. The dashed feedback arrow shows the newly predicted token being appended to the context before the next iteration. The yellow EOS check terminates the loop.
When to use it
Next-token prediction underpins all LLM inference, so there is no opt-in or opt-out. However, understanding when different sampling configurations are appropriate is directly actionable:
Tool call / JSON output: use temperature 0 (greedy) or near-0 plus constrained decoding to maximize schema conformance.
Chain-of-thought reasoning: temperature 0 is common for reproducibility; slightly higher (0.2–0.5) can improve diversity when using best-of-N sampling.
Creative writing or brainstorming: temperature 0.8–1.2 with top-p 0.95 for richer, less predictable output.
Code generation: temperature 0.2–0.4 to keep syntax valid while allowing minor variation for test diversity.
Confidence scoring / hallucination detection: any call where you enable logprobs=true to inspect per-token entropy and flag low-confidence spans.
How to implement

The following is a minimal autoregressive decode loop in pure PyTorch, showing every step explicitly. In production you would use a serving framework (vLLM, TGI) that batches requests, manages KV cache pages, and applies continuous batching—but the logic below is exactly what those systems implement internally:

import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F
from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForCausalLM


def nucleus_filter(logits: torch.Tensor, top_p: float) -> torch.Tensor:
    """Mask logits that fall outside the nucleus (top-p) set."""
    sorted_logits, sorted_idx = torch.sort(logits, descending=True)
    sorted_probs = F.softmax(sorted_logits, dim=-1)
    cumulative = torch.cumsum(sorted_probs, dim=-1)
    # Remove tokens with cumulative probability above threshold.
    # Shift right by 1 so the token that pushes above the boundary is kept.
    remove = (cumulative - sorted_probs) >= top_p
    sorted_logits[remove] = float("-inf")
    # Restore original vocabulary ordering.
    logits = torch.zeros_like(logits).scatter(1, sorted_idx, sorted_logits)
    return logits


def autoregressive_decode(
    model,
    tokenizer,
    prompt: str,
    max_new_tokens: int = 128,
    temperature: float = 0.8,
    top_p: float = 0.9,
) -> str:
    """
    Minimal next-token prediction loop.  Returns the generated text
    (excluding the prompt) as a string.
    """
    input_ids = tokenizer.encode(prompt, return_tensors="pt")  # (1, n_prompt)
    prompt_len = input_ids.shape[1]

    with torch.no_grad():
        for _ in range(max_new_tokens):
            # ---- PREFILL (first iteration) or single DECODE step ----
            outputs = model(input_ids)          # forward pass
            logits = outputs.logits[:, -1, :]   # last position: (1, vocab_size)

            # ---- SAMPLING ----
            if temperature == 0.0:
                next_id = logits.argmax(dim=-1, keepdim=True)   # greedy
            else:
                logits = logits / temperature
                logits = nucleus_filter(logits, top_p)
                probs = F.softmax(logits, dim=-1)
                next_id = torch.multinomial(probs, num_samples=1)

            input_ids = torch.cat([input_ids, next_id], dim=1)

            if next_id.item() == tokenizer.eos_token_id:
                break

    new_ids = input_ids[0, prompt_len:].tolist()
    return tokenizer.decode(new_ids, skip_special_tokens=True)


if __name__ == "__main__":
    tok = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("gpt2")
    mdl = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained("gpt2")
    result = autoregressive_decode(mdl, tok, "The agent observed that", max_new_tokens=40)
    print(result)
Examples
The following code demonstrates all four sampling strategies side by side. In practice an agent orchestrator chooses one strategy per call based on task type:

import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F


def greedy_sample(logits: torch.Tensor) -> int:
    """Argmax — always picks the single highest-probability token."""
    return int(logits.argmax(dim=-1))


def temperature_sample(logits: torch.Tensor, temperature: float) -> int:
    """Scale logits, apply softmax, then draw one sample."""
    scaled = logits / max(temperature, 1e-9)
    probs = F.softmax(scaled, dim=-1)
    return int(torch.multinomial(probs, num_samples=1))


def top_k_sample(logits: torch.Tensor, k: int = 50) -> int:
    """Zero out every logit outside the top-k; sample from the remainder."""
    topk_values, _ = torch.topk(logits, k)
    threshold = topk_values[:, -1].unsqueeze(-1)
    filtered = logits.masked_fill(logits < threshold, float("-inf"))
    probs = F.softmax(filtered, dim=-1)
    return int(torch.multinomial(probs, num_samples=1))


def nucleus_sample(logits: torch.Tensor, top_p: float = 0.9) -> int:
    """Adaptive nucleus: keep smallest set of tokens covering top_p of probability mass."""
    sorted_logits, sorted_idx = torch.sort(logits, descending=True)
    sorted_probs = F.softmax(sorted_logits, dim=-1)
    cumulative = torch.cumsum(sorted_probs, dim=-1)
    remove = (cumulative - sorted_probs) >= top_p
    sorted_logits[remove] = float("-inf")
    logits = torch.zeros_like(logits).scatter(1, sorted_idx, sorted_logits)
    probs = F.softmax(logits, dim=-1)
    return int(torch.multinomial(probs, num_samples=1))


# --- Demonstrate on a synthetic logit vector ---
torch.manual_seed(42)
batch_logits = torch.randn(1, 50257)   # GPT-2 vocab size

results = {
    "greedy":          greedy_sample(batch_logits),
    "temp_0.3":        temperature_sample(batch_logits, 0.3),
    "temp_1.2":        temperature_sample(batch_logits, 1.2),
    "top_k_40":        top_k_sample(batch_logits, k=40),
    "nucleus_p0.9":    nucleus_sample(batch_logits, top_p=0.9),
}
for strategy, token_id in results.items():
    print(f"{strategy:20s} -> token id {token_id}")

# Recommended per task type
TASK_PARAMS = {
    "json_tool_call":  {"temperature": 0.0},
    "chain_of_thought": {"temperature": 0.2, "top_p": 0.9},
    "code_generation": {"temperature": 0.3, "top_p": 0.92},
    "creative_writing": {"temperature": 0.95, "top_p": 0.95},
}
Real-world example

This example shows a complete single-step agent interaction using the OpenAI chat completions API. The logprobs feature exposes per-token probabilities, which the orchestrator uses to detect low-confidence output and decide whether to flag the result for human review:

import math
from openai import OpenAI

client = OpenAI()   # reads OPENAI_API_KEY from environment


def agent_step(
    messages: list[dict],
    model: str = "gpt-4o",
    max_tokens: int = 512,
    temperature: float = 0.0,
) -> tuple[str, float, float]:
    """
    Execute one agent step.

    Returns:
        text          -- the model's completion
        mean_logprob  -- average log P(token) across generated tokens
        min_logprob   -- lowest single-token log probability (confidence floor)
    """
    response = client.chat.completions.create(
        model=model,
        messages=messages,
        max_tokens=max_tokens,
        temperature=temperature,
        logprobs=True,
        top_logprobs=5,   # show top-5 candidates at each position
    )
    choice = response.choices[0]
    text = choice.message.content or ""

    token_logprobs = [
        lp.logprob
        for lp in (choice.logprobs.content or [])
    ]
    if token_logprobs:
        mean_lp = sum(token_logprobs) / len(token_logprobs)
        min_lp  = min(token_logprobs)
    else:
        mean_lp = min_lp = 0.0

    return text, mean_lp, min_lp


def run_research_agent(question: str) -> str:
    """Multi-step agent that flags low-confidence completions."""
    LOW_CONF_THRESHOLD = math.log(0.1)   # log(0.1) ≈ -2.30

    messages = [
        {"role": "system",  "content": "Answer concisely. End with FINAL ANSWER: <answer>"},
        {"role": "user",    "content": question},
    ]

    for step in range(8):
        text, mean_lp, min_lp = agent_step(messages)
        messages.append({"role": "assistant", "content": text})

        if min_lp < LOW_CONF_THRESHOLD:
            print(
                f"[step {step}] warning: low-confidence token "
                f"(min logprob={min_lp:.2f}, mean={mean_lp:.2f}). "
                "Consider verification or retry."
            )

        if "FINAL ANSWER:" in text:
            break

    return messages[-1]["content"]


if __name__ == "__main__":
    answer = run_research_agent(
        "What is the computational complexity of a single decode step in a transformer?"
    )
    print(answer)
Common mistakes

Expecting deterministic output without setting temperature=0 and a fixed seed. Even temperature=0 is not guaranteed deterministic across hardware, driver versions, or batch sizes due to floating-point non-associativity.

Parsing streaming JSON before generation completes. The model emits tokens one at a time; partial JSON is syntactically invalid until the closing brace or bracket appears. Buffer the full response before parsing, or use a streaming JSON parser that handles partial objects.

Treating high perplexity as a reliable hallucination signal. Low-probability tokens appear in correct factual text (e.g. proper nouns, rare units). Logprob is one signal, not a sufficient detector on its own.

Ignoring that reasoning tokens consume max_tokens budget. If the model reasons for 400 tokens and your max_tokens is 512, only 112 remain for the actual answer. Budget reasoning tokens explicitly or use separate completion calls.

Using high temperature for structured output. Even temperature 0.5 can cause the model to generate a token that opens a syntactic path it cannot close (e.g. a nested array bracket), breaking the downstream parser.
Calling the model in a tight retry loop without exponential backoff. Rate limit errors during agent decode loops should be retried with backoff; blindly retrying causes cascading quota exhaustion.
Best practices

Use structured output mode (JSON schema enforcement via constrained decoding) whenever the downstream system requires machine-readable output. It eliminates the long tail of malformed JSON far more reliably than prompting alone.
Cache system prompts and reused tool schemas at the API level (Anthropic prompt caching, OpenAI prefix caching) to convert repeated prefill cost into cheap cache hits.
Set a max_tokens budget that accounts for worst-case reasoning plus answer length, not just the expected answer. Underestimating truncates completions mid-thought silently.

Log logprobs for sampled agent completions in production. Spikes in mean perplexity correlate with distribution shift (prompt or model version change) and can trigger alerts before user-visible degradation.

Prefer nucleus sampling (top-p) over fixed top-k for open-ended generation. Top-k samples from a fixed number of candidates regardless of how concentrated or flat the distribution is; nucleus sampling adapts to distribution shape.

For high-stakes single-shot completions use best-of-N: generate N completions with temperature > 0 and select the one with highest mean logprob or a verifier score. This trades compute for reliability.
Performance & scalability
Latency breakdown
Total latency for an agent completion is the sum of prefill time plus decode time. These have different characteristics and different optimization paths:
Prefill scales as
in attention (n = context length, d = head dim) but is parallelizable across token positions. FlashAttention reduces memory to
by tiling; chunked prefill amortizes cost across batches.

Decode is sequential: each token requires one forward pass, so n_tokens decode steps take n_tokens times as long. Speculative decoding uses a small draft model to propose multiple tokens, then verifies them in parallel with the large model—achieving 2–4x speedup when acceptance rates are high.

Continuous batching (used by vLLM and TGI) packs multiple requests into the same GPU batch even when they are at different decode positions, dramatically improving GPU utilization for agent workloads with variable output lengths.

KV cache paging (PagedAttention in vLLM) stores KV cache in non-contiguous memory pages, eliminating internal fragmentation and allowing much higher memory utilization—critical when running many parallel agent instances.
Throughput vs latency trade-off

Larger batch sizes improve GPU utilization and tokens-per-second throughput but increase time-to-first-token (TTFT) because all requests in a batch must complete prefill before any decode step begins. For interactive agent loops where a human or orchestrator is waiting, optimize for TTFT; for offline batch processing, optimize for throughput.
Tooling & ecosystem
HuggingFace Transformers (
transformers
): reference implementation of autoregressive decode;
model.generate()
exposes all sampling strategies plus beam search;
GenerationConfig
serializes sampling parameters.
vLLM: production serving engine with PagedAttention, continuous batching, speculative decoding, and an OpenAI-compatible API surface. Recommended default for self-hosted deployments.
Text Generation Inference (TGI, Hugging Face): similar serving goals to vLLM with native support for Safetensors, GPTQ, and AWQ quantized models.
llama.cpp: CPU-first (with optional CUDA/Metal) inference engine using GGUF quantized models. Useful for local development, edge deployment, and testing sampling parameters without GPU access.
Outlines / Guidance (
outlines
): structured generation libraries that implement grammar-constrained decoding by masking illegal tokens at each step, making structured output from next-token prediction near-reliable.
Comparison with alternatives
Next-token prediction is not the only paradigm for sequence generation. The table below compares it with masked language modeling and diffusion language models:
Approach
Training objective
Inference pattern
Agent use case
Next-token prediction (causal LM)
Predict next token given prefix
Left-to-right autoregressive; O(n) sequential steps
All LLM agents (GPT-4, Claude, Llama, Gemini)
Masked LM (BERT-style)
Predict randomly masked tokens
Bidirectional; single forward pass; not autoregressive
Embedding, classification, re-ranking—not text generation
Diffusion LM (MDLM, etc.)
Denoise a masked or noisy token sequence
Multiple denoising passes; can refine globally
Early research; not yet production-grade for agentic tasks
Encoder-decoder (T5, BART)
Seq-to-seq with separate encoder
Autoregressive decoder; bidirectional encoder context
Translation, summarization; less common for open-ended agents
Security & reliability
Prompt injection via next-token context

Because every prior token (including tool results, retrieved documents, and user data) is part of the prediction context, malicious content inserted anywhere in the context window can shift predicted probabilities toward attacker-controlled tokens. Mitigations include sandboxing tool outputs (XML tags, clear delimiters), refusing to execute model-generated instructions that arrived via external data, and validating tool call arguments structurally before execution.
Hallucination and confidence

The model does not "know" it is wrong; it assigns probability based on pattern similarity to training data. A confidently generated (high-logprob) token can still be factually incorrect. For agentic tasks with real-world consequences (code execution, API calls, financial decisions), design the pipeline to verify outputs independently: run assertions on code, confirm API parameters against a schema, and use retrieval to ground factual claims.
Use constrained decoding for any structured output that will be parsed and executed without human review.
Treat model output as untrusted input at system boundaries—apply the same validation you would apply to user-submitted data.
Monitor token-level entropy in production; sustained entropy increases often indicate prompt distribution shift or model version changes.
Keep max_tokens bounded—runaway generation (the model repeats or loops) can produce very large outputs that consume downstream storage and processing budget.
Key takeaways
LLM generation is the repeated application of
; every agent capability—planning, tool calling, reasoning—emerges from this single objective learned at scale.
Prefill (prompt processing) is compute-bound and parallelizable; decode (token generation) is memory-bandwidth-bound and sequential—different optimization strategies apply to each.
Sampling strategy selection is not cosmetic: temperature 0 for tool calls, nucleus sampling for open-ended generation, best-of-N for high-stakes single completions.
Logprobs are a first-class diagnostic: expose them in production to detect confidence anomalies, monitor model changes, and guide retry decisions in agent loops.

Constrained decoding (grammar masks, JSON schema enforcement) is the most reliable way to guarantee structured output—it eliminates the long tail of malformed completions without expensive retry loops.
No built-in backtracking means orchestration-level error handling (retry, best-of-N, verification step) is not optional for production agents—it must be designed in from the start.
Mental model

Think of a decoder LLM as a very sophisticated autocomplete engine that has read essentially all of the internet and every book ever digitized. At each step it asks: "given everything I have seen so far, what is the single most likely next word-piece?" The magic of scale is that this simple question, trained on enough data, produces agents that appear to reason, plan, and use tools. The limit of this model is also clear: it cannot verify claims, it cannot look ahead before committing to a token, and it does not distinguish between "I know this" and "this sounds plausible." Every robustness technique in agentic AI—constrained decoding, verification loops, RAG, human-in-the-loop checkpoints—exists to compensate for precisely these limitations of the next-token prediction paradigm.
